problem_spec.description:
Optimize the yield of a Direct Arylation Reaction (DAR) benchmark using the
vendored DAR dataset. The search space is restricted to the experimentally
observed conditions present in the CSV. During simulation, the optimizer
should not see the yield column; yields are revealed only via the dataset
oracle at the human-interrupt step.

variables.name: base_SMILES
variables.description: Base encoded as a raw SMILES string present in the dataset

variables.name: ligand_SMILES
variables.description: Ligand encoded as a raw SMILES string present in the dataset

variables.name: solvent_SMILES
variables.description: Solvent encoded as a raw SMILES string present in the dataset

variables.name: concentration
variables.description: Discrete concentration levels observed in the dataset

variables.name: temperature
variables.description: Discrete temperature levels observed in the dataset

constraints: Only propose conditions that correspond to rows present in the DAR dataset.

Optimize the yield of a Direct Arylation Reaction (DAR) benchmark using the
vendored DAR dataset. The search space is restricted to the experimentally
observed conditions present in the CSV. During simulation, the optimizer
should not see the yield column; yields are revealed only via the dataset
oracle at the human-interrupt step.

kernel_config.rationale: Default Matérn-5/2 kernel selected as the safe general-purpose baseline for low-data BO. Chemistry response surfaces (yield vs. molecular descriptors) are typically moderately smooth, and Matern-5/2 provides appropriate flexibility without overfitting. This avoids the stronger smoothness prior of RBF (which may miss abrupt categorical effects) and the rougher Matern-3/2 (which may be unnecessarily pessimistic). The spectral mixture kernel (SMKBO) was considered but deferred due to limited initial data (budget=40, currently 0 observations).

bo_config.rationale: Upper Confidence Bound (UCB) selected with β=2.0 to prioritize early exploration. Rationale: (a) This is iteration 0 with zero observations—we need aggressive exploration to map the response surface; (b) Hypothesis H1 (electron push-pull matching) has high confidence and provides a strong prior, but we must still explore diverse diene/dienophile combinations to validate it and avoid premature convergence; (c) The categorical-heavy space (5 categorical variables, 26 total categories) likely contains discrete optima that pure improvement-based acquisition might miss early on; (d) UCB's explicit exploration-exploitation control allows us to dial down β in later iterations (e.g., to 1.0 or 0.5) as we transition to exploitation. LogEI was considered as the default but UCB's tunable exploration better serves the early campaign phase.

hypotheses.text: Electron-rich dienes paired with electron-poor dienophiles will yield higher DAR product yields due to favorable frontier molecular orbital interactions.
hypotheses.mechanism: The Diels-Alder reaction rate and selectivity are governed by HOMO-LUMO gap minimization; electron-rich dienes raise HOMO energy while electron-poor dienophiles lower LUMO energy, reducing the activation barrier.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Combinations with dienes bearing electron-donating groups (EDGs) and dienophiles with electron-withdrawing groups (EWGs) will show >20% higher yield than mismatched pairs in early iterations.

hypotheses.text: Steric hindrance at the diene terminus significantly reduces cycloaddition efficiency, with bulky substituents causing >30% yield decrease.
hypotheses.mechanism: The DAR requires proper orbital alignment in a compact transition state; bulky groups at the reacting termini create steric clash, increasing activation entropy and destabilizing the endo transition state.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Dienes with bulky substituents at C1 or C4 positions will consistently underperform compared to less hindered analogs across multiple solvent/base combinations.

hypotheses.text: Polar protic solvents will enhance yields for reactions involving hydrogen-bond-activated dienophiles by stabilizing the polar transition state.
hypotheses.mechanism: Protic solvents can hydrogen-bond to carbonyl or other EWGs on dienophiles, further lowering LUMO energy and stabilizing the developing partial charges in the asynchronous transition state.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Alcohol or water-containing solvent systems will show superior performance for carbonyl-substituted dienophiles compared to aprotic solvents.

hypotheses.text: Lewis acid additives will catalyze the reaction by coordinating to dienophile EWGs, but excessive Lewis acidity may cause side reactions or decomposition.
hypotheses.mechanism: Lewis acids coordinate to carbonyl/nitrile groups, further lowering dienophile LUMO energy and accelerating cycloaddition; however, strong acids may promote polymerization or diene decomposition pathways.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Moderate Lewis acids (e.g., ZnCl2, MgBr2) will show optimal yield enhancement, while strong acids (AlCl3, TiCl4) will show diminishing returns or decreased yields at higher loadings.

hypotheses.text: Cyclic dienes will exhibit higher regio- and stereoselectivity but potentially lower overall reactivity compared to acyclic dienes due to conformational constraints.
hypotheses.mechanism: Cyclic dienes are locked in s-cis conformation required for DAR, eliminating the entropic penalty of conformational pre-organization; however, ring strain and restricted orbital overlap may limit reactivity with bulky dienophiles.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Cyclopentadiene and cyclohexadiene derivatives will show faster reaction kinetics but may plateau at moderate yields due to competing oligomerization pathways.

hypotheses.text: P,N-bidentate ligands (DavePhos class) achieve superior catalytic activity at suboptimal temperatures compared to P,P-bidentate ligands (Xantphos class) due to hemilabile coordination and enhanced substrate pre-organization.
hypotheses.mechanism: The dimethylamino group in DavePhos provides hemilabile coordination that can dissociate during catalytic turnover, creating a transient coordination site for substrate binding while maintaining chelate stability. This dynamic behavior accelerates oxidative addition and/or reductive elimination steps at lower thermal energy (105°C). The P,N-chelate's 5-membered ring geometry positions the Pd center for optimal orbital overlap with substrates compared to the more rigid 6-membered P,P-chelate in Xantphos.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: DavePhos/acetate/DMAc at 120°C will exceed 90% yield, surpassing the Xantphos/acetate/DMAc benchmark of 88.41% [OBS:iter12]. The extrapolation from 78.95% at 105°C [OBS:iter20] with temperature coefficient from Xantphos (+23 points for 105→120°C) suggests 90-95% achievable.

hypotheses.text: Base steric bulk and ligand chelate architecture exhibit strong negative interaction: pivalate is incompatible with P,N-chelates but tolerated by P,P-chelates due to coordination geometry differences.
hypotheses.mechanism: P,N-chelates require flexible coordination sphere dynamics for hemilabile behavior. Pivalate's tert-butyl group creates steric congestion at the Pd center when combined with the amino directing group, disrupting the hemilabile equilibrium and/or blocking substrate access. P,P-chelates present a more open coordination hemisphere that accommodates pivalate's bulk without catastrophic activity loss (65.37% vs 88.41% for Xantphos at 105°C, [OBS:iter13 vs iter12]).
hypotheses.testable_prediction: No P,N-ligand/pivalate combination will exceed 60% yield regardless of temperature or solvent. Systematic exploration of P,N-ligands (QPhos, tBuDavePhos, etc.) with acetate will consistently outperform pivalate by >40 percentage points.

hypotheses.text: Polar aprotic amide solvents (DMAc, DMF) provide specific solvation stabilization for acetate-base Pd-catalyzed systems through hydrogen-bond-acceptor interactions with the Pd-bound acetate.
hypotheses.mechanism: DMAc/DMF's strong hydrogen-bond-acceptor character (O=C-NMe2) stabilizes the Pd-acetate bond in the catalytic cycle, preventing premature proton transfer or acetate dissociation. This specific interaction is less critical for pivalate due to its lower acidity and stronger Pd-O bond. Non-amide polar solvents (esters, p-xylene) show 15-25 point yield penalties [OBS:iter16, iter17, iter19] because they lack the optimal H-bond acceptor geometry.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Systematic variation of amide solvents (NMP, DMI, DMAc, DMF) will show <10 point yield variation, confirming the amide functional group as the critical solvation motif. Non-amide polar solvents will consistently underperform by >15 points regardless of ligand or base.

hypotheses.text: Temperature optimization is ligand-class specific: P,N-chelates exhibit lower activation energy requirements and may show yield plateaus or decomposition at temperatures above 120°C where P,P-chelates continue improving.
hypotheses.mechanism: P,N-chelates' hemilabile coordination enables lower-energy catalytic pathways, achieving near-maximum turnover at 105-120°C. Further temperature increases provide diminishing returns and may promote P,N bond cleavage or phosphine oxidation. P,P-chelates require higher thermal energy to overcome rigid chelate constraints, showing stronger temperature dependence (23 point gain for 105→120°C [OBS:iter13 vs iter12]).
hypotheses.testable_prediction: DavePhos/acetate/DMAc will show <5 point improvement from 120→130°C, while Xantphos/acetate/DMAc will show >10 point improvement over the same range. Optimal temperature for P,N-chelates is 115-125°C; for P,P-chelates is 125-135°C.

hypotheses.text: The surrogate model's persistent failure (ModelFittingError across iterations 18-21) stems from embedding collapse when representing chemically distinct phosphine ligand architectures (monodentate vs bidentate, P,P vs P,N) in continuous vector space.
hypotheses.mechanism: Fingerprint-based embeddings (Morgan/ECFP) encode local atomic environments but fail to capture global molecular properties critical for catalysis: denticity, bite angle, hemilability, and chelate ring size. These architectures produce similar fingerprint vectors despite radically different catalytic behavior, creating non-smooth response surfaces that violate GP stationarity assumptions. The model cannot learn that monodentate → 0% yield and P,N-chelate → high yield from fingerprint similarity alone.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Reconfiguration to structured kernels (categorical for ligand class, continuous for temperature) or descriptor-based embeddings (capturing denticity, Tolman cone angle, bite angle) will restore model stability and predictive accuracy. Random exploration fallback rate will decrease from 100% (iter 18-21) to <20%.

hypotheses.text: P,N-bidentate ligands (DavePhos class) achieve superior catalytic activity at suboptimal temperatures compared to P,P-bidentate ligands (Xantphos class) due to hemilabile coordination and enhanced substrate pre-organization.
hypotheses.mechanism: The dimethylamino group in DavePhos provides hemilabile coordination that can dissociate during catalytic turnover, creating a transient coordination site for substrate binding while maintaining chelate stability. This dynamic behavior accelerates oxidative addition and/or reductive elimination steps at lower thermal energy (105°C). The P,N-chelate's 5-membered ring geometry positions the Pd center for optimal orbital overlap with substrates compared to the more rigid 6-membered P,P-chelate in Xantphos.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: DavePhos/acetate/DMAc at 120°C will exceed 90% yield, surpassing the Xantphos/acetate/DMAc benchmark of 88.41% [OBS:iter12]. The extrapolation from 78.95% at 105°C [OBS:iter20] with temperature coefficient from Xantphos (+23 points for 105→120°C) suggests 90-95% achievable. Pivalate is excluded based on catastrophic 50-point drop at iter21.

hypotheses.text: Base steric bulk and ligand chelate architecture are strongly coupled: pivalate requires rigid P,P-bidentate chelation for thermal stability, while acetate enables dynamic P,N-bidentate pathways.
hypotheses.mechanism: Pivalate's steric bulk (tert-butyl) creates a crowded Pd coordination sphere that demands the rigid, wide bite-angle geometry of Xantphos (111°) to maintain catalytic competence. The more flexible P,N-chelate in DavePhos cannot accommodate pivalate's steric demands, leading to catalyst decomposition or off-cycle resting states (observed as 28.64% yield vs 78.95% with acetate, -50.3 points [OBS:iter21 vs iter20]). Acetate's smaller steric profile is compatible with both P,P and P,N architectures.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Xantphos/pivalate/DMAc at 120°C will achieve >75% yield (consistent with prior pivalate/DMAc successes), while DavePhos/pivalate will remain <40% at any temperature. This validates architectural compatibility as a design constraint distinct from individual component optimization.

hypotheses.text: Solvent polarity requirements are mediated by base identity: acetate enables productive catalysis in low-polarity media (p-xylene, esters), while pivalate strictly requires high-polarity amide solvents.
hypotheses.mechanism: Acetate's compact size and diffuse negative charge enable effective ion pairing and solvent separation in media with modest dielectric constants (ε~2-6, p-xylene and esters). Pivalate's localized charge and steric bulk demand strong solvation by polar aprotic solvents (DMAc, DMF, ε~37) to maintain catalytic activity. The 67% yield in p-xylene with acetate [OBS:iter16] vs unobserved (but predicted failing) pivalate/p-xylene supports this mechanism.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: DavePhos/acetate/p-xylene at 120°C will achieve 50-65% yield, demonstrating that P,N-chelate compatibility extends to non-polar solvents with acetate. DavePhos/pivalate/p-xylene will achieve <10%, confirming base-solvent coupling. This expands the solvent design space for P,N-ligand systems.

hypotheses.text: The GP surrogate's persistent ModelFittingError stems from embedding collapse: fingerprint_concat representations cannot distinguish chemically distinct ligand architectures (monodentate vs bidentate, P,P vs P,N) that have radically different catalytic outcomes.
hypotheses.mechanism: Morgan fingerprints capture local atomic environments but fail to encode global molecular properties critical for catalysis: chelate ring size, bite angle, hemilability, and donor atom identity. The 0% yield with monodentate tri-tert-butylphosphine [OBS:iter18] and 78.95% with bidentate DavePhos [OBS:iter20] are chemically distant but fingerprint-similar, creating non-smooth response surfaces that violate GP stationarity assumptions. The embedding collapses distinct chemical behaviors into similar vectors.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Reconfiguration to a categorical kernel encoding ligand class (monodentate/P,P-bidentate/P,N-bidentate) as explicit features will resolve ModelFittingError and restore reliable uncertainty quantification. Alternatively, structured embeddings (e.g., reaction-based fingerprints, ligand parameter descriptors like %Vbur, Tolman electronic parameter) will improve surrogate performance.

hypotheses.text: Temperature optimization for P,N-ligand systems follows a shallower response surface than P,P-systems, enabling robust operation across 105-120°C without steep penalties.
hypotheses.mechanism: The hemilabile coordination in DavePhos provides an internal temperature compensation mechanism: at lower temperatures, the P,N-chelate remains intact providing stability; at higher temperatures, amino dissociation creates coordinative unsaturation for substrate activation. This dynamic equilibrium buffers the system against temperature variation. In contrast, Xantphos' rigid P,P-chelate lacks this adaptability, showing steep temperature sensitivity (65.37% at 105°C vs 88.41% at 120°C, -23 points [OBS:iter13 vs iter12]).
hypotheses.testable_prediction: DavePhos/acetate/DMAc at 110°C and 115°C will show <10 point variation in yield (70-85% range), versus the >20 point variation observed for Xantphos. This operational robustness is valuable for scale-up where precise temperature control is challenging.

hypotheses.text: The fingerprint_concat embedding creates chemically nonsensical similarity metrics that collapse distinct ligand architectures (monodentate vs P,P-bidentate vs P,N-bidentate) into similar vectors, causing GP kernel assumptions to fail catastrophically.
hypotheses.mechanism: Morgan fingerprints encode local atomic environments but cannot capture global chelation topology—bite angle, ring size, hemilability, and donor atom identity. The 0% yield with monodentate tri-tert-butylphosphine [OBS:iter18] and 78.95% with bidentate DavePhos [OBS:iter20] are chemically distant but fingerprint-similar, creating a discontinuous response surface that violates GP stationarity. The kernel assumes smoothness in embedding space where none exists chemically.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Reconfiguration to categorical one-hot encoding for ligand_class (monodentate/P,P-bidentate/P,N-bidentate) as explicit kernel features will resolve ModelFittingError. Alternatively, structured descriptors (%Vbur, Tolman electronic parameter, bite angle) will restore reliable uncertainty quantification. Test: if reconfiguration succeeds, GP fitting will converge within 3 iterations.

hypotheses.text: The LLM selection process exhibits systematic SMILES parsing failures when ligand structures share superficial similarity (phosphine cores with phenyl substituents) but differ in critical functional groups (dimethylamino vs methoxy directing groups).
hypotheses.mechanism: The selection rationale for iteration 22 misidentified Brettphos-analog (P(Cy)2-Ar-OMe, P,P-bidentate with electron-withdrawing methoxy) as DavePhos (P(tBu)2-Ar-NMe2, P,N-bidentate with electron-donating amino). Both are bulky phosphines with ortho-substituted biaryl backbones, but the directing group chemistry is inverted. The LLM pattern-matches on structural scaffolds without validating functional group identity, leading to catastrophic chemical reasoning errors.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Implementation of explicit ligand classification tags (ligand_family: DavePhos/Xantphos/Brettphos/PCy3/etc.) in the dataset will prevent parsing errors. All future selections must validate directing group identity (NMe2 vs OMe vs H) before chemical reasoning. Test: zero misidentification errors in next 5 iterations.

hypotheses.text: P,N-bidentate ligands (DavePhos class) with acetate base in DMAc at 120°C will exceed 90% yield, establishing a new benchmark superior to the current best of 88.41% with Xantphos.
hypotheses.mechanism: DavePhos achieved 78.95% at 105°C with acetate [OBS:iter20], outperforming Xantphos at identical temperature (65.37% [OBS:iter13]) by 13.6 points. The P,N-chelate's hemilabile coordination enables lower-energy catalytic pathways. Extrapolating the Xantphos temperature coefficient (+23 points for 105→120°C [OBS:iter12 vs iter13]) to DavePhos suggests 90-95% achievable at 120°C. Pivalate is excluded due to catastrophic incompatibility (28.64% vs 78.95%, -50.3 points [OBS:iter21 vs iter20]).
hypotheses.testable_prediction: DavePhos/acetate/DMAc/0.1M/120°C will achieve 90-95% yield. This requires manual override or corrected shortlist generation, as the surrogate remains in failure mode. Success will validate P,N-architecture as the optimal ligand class for this DAR system.

hypotheses.text: The mixed sum-product kernel with explicit categorical handling will outperform single-kernel GP configurations by correctly modeling the interaction between ligand_class, base_identity, and solvent_class as distinct feature types.
hypotheses.mechanism: Prior configurations (Matern-5/2, Matern-3/2) assumed a unified metric space over fingerprint_concat embeddings, failing because chemical categories (ligand architecture, base sterics, solvent polarity) have heterogeneous similarity structures. A mixed kernel applies RBF to continuous variables (temperature, concentration) and categorical kernels (Hamming, one-hot RBF) to discrete variables, respecting the problem structure.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Mixed kernel configuration will converge without ModelFittingError and provide reliable uncertainty estimates (predicted variance <15) for in-distribution predictions. Temperature and concentration will show smooth RBF correlation; ligand/base/solvent will show discrete categorical effects. Test: successful GP fitting on iter 1-22 data within 2 attempts.

hypotheses.text: The campaign can recover from the current failure mode by prioritizing exploitation of the validated DavePhos/acetate/DMAc system at 120°C over continued exploration of underperforming regions.
hypotheses.mechanism: With 18 iterations remaining and a clear path to >90% yield identified (DavePhos/acetate/DMAc/120°C), the optimal strategy shifts from exploration (which has produced 11 stagnant iterations and catastrophic failures) to targeted exploitation. The embedding/surrogate issues prevent reliable exploration anyway; manual chemical guidance toward the known optimum is risk-minimizing.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Executing DavePhos/acetate/DMAc/0.1M/120°C will achieve >90% yield within 1-2 iterations, establishing a new best and validating recovery. Subsequent iterations can cautiously explore neighboring conditions (115°C, 125°C, 0.05M, 0.2M) with the working surrogate. Test: yield >90% on next exploitation iteration.

hypotheses.text: The fingerprint_concat embedding creates chemically nonsensical similarity metrics that collapse distinct ligand architectures (monodentate vs P,P-bidentate vs P,N-bidentate) into similar vectors, causing GP kernel assumptions to fail catastrophically.
hypotheses.mechanism: Morgan fingerprints encode local atomic environments but cannot capture global chelation topology—bite angle, ring size, hemilability, and donor atom identity. The 0% yield with monodentate tri-tert-butylphosphine [OBS:iter18] and 78.95% with bidentate DavePhos [OBS:iter20] are chemically distant but fingerprint-similar, creating a discontinuous response surface that violates GP stationarity. The kernel assumes smoothness in embedding space where none exists chemically.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Reconfiguration to categorical one-hot encoding for ligand_class (monodentate/P,P-bidentate/P,N-bidentate) as explicit kernel features will resolve ModelFittingError. Alternatively, structured descriptors (%Vbur, Tolman electronic parameter, bite angle) will enable successful GP fitting.

hypotheses.text: P,N-bidentate ligands (DavePhos class) exhibit superior activity at suboptimal temperatures compared to P,P-bidentate ligands (Xantphos class) due to hemilabile coordination and flexible chelate ring geometry.
hypotheses.mechanism: The 5-membered P,N-chelate in DavePhos allows partial decoordination during catalytic turnover, reducing entropic penalties in the rate-determining step. The rigid 6-membered P,P-chelate in Xantphos provides static stabilization but restricts conformational freedom. At 105°C, DavePhos achieved 78.95% vs Xantphos 65.37% [OBS:iter20 vs iter13], a 13.6-point advantage that diminishes at 120°C where thermal energy overcomes entropic barriers.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: DavePhos/acetate/DMAc at 120°C will achieve 90-95% yield, exceeding the 88.41% Xantphos benchmark. Temperature coefficient for P,N-ligands will be shallower than P,P-ligands (<15 points for 15°C change vs 23 points observed for Xantphos).

hypotheses.text: Base steric bulk and ligand chelate flexibility are strongly coupled—pivalate's tert-butyl group is incompatible with flexible P,N-chelates but tolerated by rigid P,P-chelates.
hypotheses.mechanism: Pivalate's steric bulk requires specific coordination geometry for productive ion pairing with the Pd center. The rigid Xantphos backbone enforces a consistent coordination environment that accommodates pivalate (80.61% at 120°C [OBS:iter8]), while the flexible DavePhos P,N-chelate cannot maintain optimal geometry with bulky pivalate, causing catalytic cycle collapse (28.64% [OBS:iter21]). Acetate's smaller size is compatible with both architectures.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Xantphos/pivalate will maintain 75-80% yield across 105-120°C, showing base-tolerant robustness. DavePhos/pivalate will remain <40% regardless of temperature, confirming architectural incompatibility. Brettphos (rigid P,P with electron-withdrawing groups) will show intermediate behavior with pivalate.

hypotheses.text: Electron-deficient phosphine ligands (Brettphos-analog with CF3 groups) are fundamentally incompatible with Pd(0) oxidative addition in this DAR system, regardless of denticity or solvent conditions.
hypotheses.mechanism: The catalytic cycle requires electron-rich Pd(0) for oxidative addition to the aryl halide substrate. Electron-withdrawing CF3 substituents on the phosphine reduce electron density at Pd, increasing the activation barrier for oxidative addition beyond accessible thermal energy. The 0.07% yield with Brettphos-analog [OBS:iter22] reflects complete cycle failure at the first step, not optimization failure.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: No combination of base, solvent, or temperature will achieve >10% yield with electron-deficient phosphines (Brettphos, tBuBrettphos, analogs with σp > 0.2). Electron-rich variants (tBu, OMe, NMe2 substituents) will restore activity if denticity is maintained.

hypotheses.text: The 88.41% benchmark represents a local optimum within the Xantphos/P,P-chelate architectural class, while the true global optimum lies in the DavePhos/P,N-chelate class at higher temperatures.
hypotheses.mechanism: Xantphos optimization has plateaued at 88.41% with limited exploration of concentration (fixed at 0.1M) and no further ligand modifications within P,P-class. DavePhos has only been tested at 105°C with acetate (78.95%) and pivalate (28.64%). Extrapolating Xantphos temperature coefficient (+23 points for 105→120°C) to DavePhos predicts 90-95% at 120°C, but P,N-ligands may exhibit different temperature response due to hemilability.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: DavePhos/acetate/DMAc/0.1M/120°C will achieve 92-97% yield, establishing new global optimum. Systematic concentration variation (0.05-0.2M) with DavePhos at 120°C may reveal additional 3-5 point gains through optimal ion pairing.

hypotheses.text: Categorical one-hot encoding of ligand_class (monodentate/P,P-bidentate/P,N-bidentate) will resolve the persistent ModelFittingError by restoring chemically meaningful similarity metrics to the GP kernel.
hypotheses.mechanism: Morgan fingerprint_concat embeddings collapse distinct chelation topologies into similar vectors—monodentate PCy3 (7.26% yield) and bidentate DavePhos (78.95% yield) appear fingerprint-similar despite 71.7-point outcome difference. This creates a discontinuous response surface violating GP stationarity. Explicit categorical features enable the kernel to model distinct ligand classes as separate response surfaces with appropriate uncertainty.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Reconfiguration to categorical ligand_class encoding will enable successful GP fitting within 2 iterations, with predicted uncertainties <20 for in-distribution points. The model will correctly predict <10% yield for monodentate candidates and >70% for bidentate candidates with appropriate confidence intervals.

hypotheses.text: DavePhos/acetate/DMAc/0.1M/120°C will achieve 90-95% yield, establishing a new benchmark exceeding the current Xantphos best of 88.41%.
hypotheses.mechanism: DavePhos achieved 78.95% at 105°C [OBS:iter20], outperforming Xantphos at identical temperature (65.37% [OBS:iter13]) by 13.6 points due to hemilabile P,N-chelation. The Xantphos temperature coefficient (+23 points for 105→120°C [OBS:iter12 vs iter13]) extrapolates DavePhos to 90-95% at 120°C. Pivalate is excluded due to catastrophic incompatibility (-50.3 points [OBS:iter21 vs iter20]).
hypotheses.testable_prediction: DavePhos/acetate/DMAc/0.1M/120°C will achieve 90-95% yield. This is the highest-confidence untested condition. Success validates P,N-architecture as optimal; failure indicates unexpected temperature sensitivity or decomposition pathway.

hypotheses.text: Temperature optimization is ligand-class specific: P,N-chelates exhibit shallower temperature response and may plateau or decompose above 120°C, while P,P-chelates continue improving through 130°C.
hypotheses.mechanism: P,N-chelates' hemilabile coordination enables low-energy catalytic pathways, achieving near-maximum turnover at 105-120°C. Further heating may promote P-N bond cleavage or phosphine oxidation. P,P-chelates require higher thermal energy to overcome rigid chelate constraints, showing stronger temperature dependence (23-point gain for 15°C [OBS:iter13 vs iter12]).
hypotheses.testable_prediction: DavePhos/acetate/DMAc will show <5-point improvement from 120→130°C, while Xantphos/acetate/DMAc will show >10-point improvement. Optimal P,N temperature is 115-125°C; optimal P,P temperature is 125-135°C.

hypotheses.text: Acetate enables productive catalysis in low-polarity solvents (p-xylene, esters) with P,N-ligands, while pivalate strictly requires high-polarity amide solvents regardless of ligand architecture.
hypotheses.mechanism: Acetate's compact size and diffuse charge enable effective ion pairing in media with modest dielectric constants (ε~2-6). Pivalate's localized charge and steric bulk demand strong solvation by polar aprotic solvents (ε~37). The 67% yield in p-xylene with acetate/Xantphos [OBS:iter16] demonstrates this flexibility; DavePhos/acetate/p-xylene is untested but predicted viable based on ligand similarity.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: DavePhos/acetate/p-xylene at 120°C will achieve 50-65% yield, expanding the solvent design space. DavePhos/pivalate/p-xylene will achieve <10%, confirming base-solvent coupling is ligand-independent.

hypotheses.text: The mixed sum-product kernel with explicit categorical handling for ligand_class, base_identity, and solvent_class will outperform single-kernel configurations by correctly modeling heterogeneous feature interactions.
hypotheses.mechanism: Prior Matern-5/2 configurations assumed unified metric space over fingerprint embeddings, failing because chemical categories have distinct similarity structures. A mixed kernel applies RBF to continuous variables (temperature, concentration) and categorical kernels (Hamming, one-hot) to discrete variables, respecting problem structure and enabling reliable uncertainty quantification.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Mixed kernel configuration will converge without ModelFittingError. Temperature/concentration will show smooth RBF correlation; ligand/base/solvent will show discrete categorical effects with interpretable feature importances.

hypotheses.text: P,N-bidentate ligands (DavePhos class) achieve >90% yield at 120°C with acetate/DMAc, surpassing the current 88.41% benchmark held by P,P-bidentate Xantphos.
hypotheses.mechanism: The hemilabile dimethylamino group in DavePhos enables dynamic coordination that accelerates catalytic turnover relative to rigid P,P-chelates. At 120°C, the 23-point temperature coefficient observed for Xantphos (105→120°C) applied to DavePhos's 78.95% at 105°C predicts 90-95% yield. The P,N-architecture's superior performance at 105°C (13.6 points above Xantphos) indicates intrinsic electronic/steric advantages that should persist or amplify at optimal temperature.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: DavePhos/acetate/DMAc/0.1M/120°C will achieve 90-95% yield, exceeding the 88.41% benchmark by 2-7 percentage points. The yield gain will be statistically significant (p<0.05) if replicate experiments are performed.

hypotheses.text: Base steric bulk exhibits strong coupling with ligand architecture: pivalate is incompatible with P,N-chelates but viable with rigid P,P-chelates.
hypotheses.mechanism: The flexible P,N-chelate in DavePhos creates a sterically congested active site that cannot accommodate pivalate's bulk (tert-butyl + two methyls). The 50.3-point yield collapse (78.95%→28.64%) with pivalate/DavePhos vs minimal effect with Xantphos indicates that rigid P,P-backbones tolerate base variation better than hemilabile P,N-systems. Pivalate's thermal stability advantages are only accessible with appropriate ligand selection.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Xantphos/pivalate/DMAc/120°C will achieve >80% yield (within 8 points of Xantphos/acetate), while DavePhos/pivalate at any temperature remains <35%. The ligand-base interaction term in any statistical model will be significant (p<0.01).

hypotheses.text: Categorical one-hot encoding of ligand_class (monodentate/P,P-bidentate/P,N-bidentate) will resolve persistent ModelFittingError by restoring chemically meaningful similarity metrics.
hypotheses.mechanism: Morgan fingerprint_concat embeddings collapse distinct chelation topologies into similar vectors—monodentate PCy3 (7.26% yield) and bidentate DavePhos (78.95% yield) appear fingerprint-similar despite 71.7-point outcome difference. This creates a discontinuous response surface violating GP stationarity. Explicit categorical features enable the kernel to model distinct ligand classes as separate response surfaces with appropriate uncertainty.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Reconfiguration to categorical ligand_class encoding will enable successful GP fitting within 2 iterations, with predicted uncertainties <20 for in-distribution points. The model will correctly predict <10% yield for monodentate candidates and >70% for bidentate candidates with appropriate confidence intervals.

hypotheses.text: Temperature response is shallower for P,N-bidentate ligands than P,P-bidentate ligands due to preorganized hemilabile coordination.
hypotheses.mechanism: Xantphos requires thermal activation (105→120°C: +23 points) to achieve optimal geometry for catalytic turnover. DavePhos's built-in hemilability enables productive coordination at lower temperatures, reducing the temperature coefficient. The 78.95% yield at 105°C vs 65.37% for Xantphos suggests the P,N-system is already near its thermal optimum at 105°C.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: DavePhos temperature coefficient (90→105→120°C) will be <15 points total, versus 23+ points for Xantphos. DavePhos at 90°C will achieve >65% (vs 55.17% for Xantphos at 90°C), and the 120°C improvement over 105°C will be <10 points (vs 23 for Xantphos).

hypotheses.text: Concentration optimization can partially compensate for suboptimal temperature in energy-efficient process design.
hypotheses.mechanism: Episode E14 demonstrated that 0.153M pivalate/Xantphos at 90°C achieved 60.68% vs 55.17% at 0.1M—a +5.5 point concentration effect. With P,N-ligands showing superior low-temperature performance, concentration tuning may enable >75% yield at 90°C, approaching the 88.41% thermal optimum with substantial energy savings.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: DavePhos/acetate/DMAc at 0.15-0.2M/90°C will achieve 70-80% yield, representing a 13-23 point improvement over 0.1M/90°C and closing 50-70% of the gap to the 120°C optimum. The concentration effect will be statistically significant (p<0.05).

hypotheses.text: Categorical one-hot encoding of ligand_class (monodentate/P,P-bidentate/P,N-bidentate) will resolve persistent ModelFittingError by restoring chemically meaningful similarity metrics to the GP surrogate.
hypotheses.mechanism: Morgan fingerprint_concat embeddings collapse distinct chelation topologies into similar vectors—monodentate PCy3 (7.26% yield [OBS:iter24]) and bidentate DavePhos (78.95% yield [OBS:iter20]) appear fingerprint-similar despite 71.7-point outcome difference. This creates a discontinuous response surface violating GP stationarity assumptions. Explicit categorical features enable the kernel to model distinct ligand classes as separate response surfaces with appropriate uncertainty, respecting the mechanistic reality that denticity is a discrete categorical variable, not a continuous spectrum.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Reconfiguration to categorical ligand_class encoding will enable successful GP fitting within 2 iterations, with predicted uncertainties <20 for in-distribution points. The model will correctly predict <10% yield for monodentate candidates and >70% for bidentate candidates with appropriate confidence intervals. No ModelFittingError will occur for the remaining 15 iterations.

DavePhos achieved 78.95% at 105°C [OBS:iter20], outperforming Xantphos at identical temperature (65.37% [OBS:iter13]) by 13.6 points. hypotheses.testable_prediction: DavePhos/acetate/DMAc/0.1M/120°C will achieve 90-95% yield, exceeding the 88.41% benchmark by 2-7 percentage points. This is the highest-priority test for the remaining 15 iterations. Success validates P,N-architecture as the optimal ligand class for this DAR system.

hypotheses.text: Base steric bulk and ligand chelate architecture are strongly coupled—pivalate is incompatible with flexible P,N-chelates but viable with rigid P,P-chelates.
hypotheses.mechanism: The flexible P,N-chelate in DavePhos creates a sterically congested active site that cannot accommodate pivalate's bulk (tert-butyl + two methyls). The 50.3-point yield collapse (78.95%→28.64% [OBS:iter20 vs iter21]) with pivalate/DavePhos versus minimal effect with Xantphos indicates that rigid P,P-backbones tolerate base variation better than hemilabile P,N-systems. Pivalate's thermal stability advantages are only accessible with appropriate ligand selection. This interaction is categorical, not continuous.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Xantphos/pivalate/DMAc/120°C will achieve >80% yield (within 8 points of Xantphos/acetate), while DavePhos/pivalate at any temperature remains <35%. The ligand-base interaction term will be statistically significant (p<0.01) in any regression model.

hypotheses.text: Cs+ cation is mechanistically excluded from productive catalysis in this DAR system, causing catastrophic yield collapse regardless of ligand or solvent optimization.
hypotheses.mechanism: The 4.74% yield with Cs+ acetate and electron-rich dimethoxy-Brettphos [OBS:iter25] confirms that Cs+ is not merely suboptimal but fundamentally incompatible. Despite correcting the electron-deficiency problem that caused 0.07% yield with CF3-Brettphos [OBS:iter22], the Cs+ cation penalty completely nullified any potential ligand improvement. Cs+ likely causes catalyst decomposition, inactive resting state formation, or disrupted ion pairing. The 32.5-point K+ advantage [RULE:R5] reflects a mechanistic constraint, not a tunable parameter.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: No Cs+ combination will exceed 15% yield regardless of ligand (monodentate, P,P, or P,N), solvent, or temperature. Systematic exclusion of Cs+ from the search space will improve optimization efficiency by eliminating a categorical failure mode. The K+/Cs+ effect will be modeled as a categorical variable with near-deterministic outcomes.

hypotheses.mechanism: Morgan fingerprint_concat embeddings collapse distinct chelation topologies into similar vectors—monodentate PCy3 (7.26% yield [OBS:iter24]) and bidentate DavePhos (78.95% yield [OBS:iter20]) appear fingerprint-similar despite 71.7-point outcome difference. This creates a discontinuous response surface violating GP stationarity assumptions. Explicit categorical features enable the kernel to model distinct ligand classes as separate response surfaces with appropriate uncertainty, respecting the mechanistic reality that denticity is a discrete categorical variable, not a continuous spectrum.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Reconfiguration to categorical ligand_class encoding will enable successful GP fitting within 2 iterations, with predicted uncertainties <20 for in-distribution points and acquisition function values that correlate with expected yields (DavePhos/acetate/120°C/K+ should have highest acquisition value).

hypotheses.text: DavePhos (P,N-bidentate) with K+ acetate at 120°C will achieve >90% yield, exceeding the current 88.41% benchmark by combining the superior P,N-chelate architecture with optimal temperature.
hypotheses.mechanism: DavePhos achieved 78.95% at 105°C [OBS:iter20] versus Xantphos 65.37% at 105°C [OBS:iter13]—a 13.6-point P,N advantage at suboptimal temperature. Xantphos gained +23 points (65.37% → 88.41%) from 105°C → 120°C [OBS:iter12]. Assuming similar temperature sensitivity for P,N-chelates, DavePhos at 120°C should achieve 78.95% + 15-20 points = 93-99%. The P,N-hemilabile coordination enables more efficient oxidative addition and reductive elimination at elevated temperatures.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: DavePhos/K+ acetate/DMAc/0.1M/120°C will achieve 90-95% yield, establishing a new campaign benchmark and validating P,N-bidentate superiority over P,P-architectures.

hypotheses.text: Cation identity (K+ vs Cs+) dominates over ligand electronic tuning—Cs+ systematically suppresses yield by 20-30 points regardless of ligand architecture.
hypotheses.mechanism: The 4.74% yield with Cs+ acetate and electron-rich Brettphos-analog [OBS:iter25] versus 78.95% with K+ acetate and DavePhos [OBS:iter20] demonstrates cation effects overwhelm ligand optimization. Rule R5 establishes +32.5 point K+ advantage [confidence 0.99]. Cs+ larger ionic radius (1.67Å vs 1.38Å) disrupts precatalyst activation and transmetalation energetics through altered ion pairing and coordination geometry, creating a mechanistic bottleneck that ligand tuning cannot overcome.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Any condition with Cs+ will achieve <30% yield regardless of ligand (bidentate or monodentate), solvent, or temperature, while equivalent K+ conditions will achieve >50% with bidentate ligands.

hypotheses.text: Base-ligand coupling is specific to P,N-chelates—pivalate causes catastrophic failure with DavePhos (-50.3 points, 78.95% → 28.64% [OBS:iter21]) but only modest penalty with Xantphos (-7.8 points, 88.41% → 80.61%).
hypotheses.mechanism: P,N-chelates (DavePhos) have flexible hemilabile coordination that requires specific anion geometry for optimal metal center accessibility. Pivalate's steric bulk disrupts the dynamic coordination equilibrium essential for P,N-catalytic cycles. P,P-chelates (Xantphos) have rigid bite angles that are less sensitive to anion sterics, tolerating pivalate with minimal penalty. This creates a base-ligand interaction term that must be modeled as a categorical product in the kernel.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: With reconfigured categorical encoding, the GP will learn a strong negative interaction coefficient for (pivalate × P,N-chelate) and near-zero interaction for (pivalate × P,P-chelate), enabling accurate yield predictions for base-ligand combinations.

hypotheses.text: Monodentate phosphines are mechanistically excluded from productive catalysis in this DAR system, with yields <10% regardless of steric/electronic tuning.
hypotheses.mechanism: Monodentate phosphines (tri-tert-butylphosphine 0% [OBS:iter18], PCy3 7.26% [OBS:iter24], PPh3 expected 0-5%) cannot stabilize the required Pd(0)/Pd(II) redox cycle in the DAR mechanism. Bidentate chelation is essential for preventing Pd-black precipitation and maintaining catalytically active species. The 0-7% range represents background reactivity from unliganded Pd or trace bidentate impurities, not productive monodentate catalysis.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: All monodentate candidates in remaining iterations will achieve <10% yield; the GP with categorical encoding will learn to assign near-zero probability to monodentate ligand_class regardless of other variables.

hypotheses.text: Categorical one-hot encoding of ligand_class (monodentate/P,P-bidentate/P,N-bidentate) will resolve persistent ModelFittingError by restoring chemically meaningful similarity metrics.
hypotheses.mechanism: Morgan fingerprint_concat embeddings collapse distinct chelation topologies into similar vectors—monodentate PCy3 (7.26% yield [OBS:iter24]) and bidentate DavePhos (78.95% yield [OBS:iter20]) appear fingerprint-similar despite 71.7-point outcome difference. This creates a discontinuous response surface violating GP stationarity. Explicit categorical features enable the kernel to model distinct ligand classes as separate response surfaces with appropriate uncertainty. The 82.09% DavePhos result at 120°C [OBS:iter26] further validates that denticity is a discrete categorical variable, not continuous.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Reconfiguration to categorical ligand_class encoding will enable successful GP fitting within 2 iterations, with predicted uncertainties <20 for in-distribution points. The model will correctly predict <10% yield for monodentate candidates and >70% for bidentate candidates with appropriate confidence intervals.

hypotheses.text: The 88.41% Xantphos benchmark represents the practical optimum for this DAR system; DavePhos cannot exceed it due to P,N-chelate thermal instability above 120°C.
hypotheses.mechanism: DavePhos achieved 82.09% at 120°C [OBS:iter26], only +3.14 points over 105°C [OBS:iter20], versus Xantphos's +23 points for the same temperature range [OBS:iter12 vs iter13]. The shallow P,N temperature response suggests the 78.95%→82.09% gain is near the P,N thermal optimum, with decomposition or ligand degradation limiting further improvement. The rigid P,P-chelate in Xantphos provides superior thermal stability, enabling higher effective turnover at 120°C. The 6.32-point gap to benchmark may be intrinsic to ligand architecture, not optimizable.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: DavePhos at 125-130°C will show yield decline (<80%) due to thermal degradation, confirming 120°C as P,N optimum. Xantphos at 125-130°C will maintain or improve yield (88-92%), demonstrating P,P thermal robustness. The global optimum remains Xantphos/acetate/DMAc/120°C at 88.41%.

hypotheses.text: Concentration optimization with DavePhos at 120°C can close the 6.32-point gap to the Xantphos benchmark through improved ion pairing dynamics.
hypotheses.mechanism: All high-yield DavePhos results use 0.1M concentration [OBS:iter20: 78.95%, OBS:iter26: 82.09%]. Episode E14 demonstrated +5.5 point gain from 0.1M→0.153M with Xantphos/pivalate at 90°C. DavePhos's hemilabile coordination may be more sensitive to ionic strength, with optimal Pd-acetate ion pairing at 0.15-0.2M. The 82.09% result at 0.1M/120°C suggests headroom for improvement through concentration tuning rather than temperature.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: DavePhos/acetate/DMAc at 0.15-0.2M/120°C will achieve 86-90% yield, closing 60-90% of the gap to 88.41% benchmark. The concentration effect will be statistically significant (p<0.05) and larger for P,N-chelates than observed for P,P-chelates.

hypotheses.text: Mixed sum-product kernel with explicit categorical handling will outperform single-kernel configurations by correctly modeling base-ligand coupling as a categorical interaction term.
hypotheses.mechanism: The pivalate penalty is ligand-class dependent: -50.3 points with DavePhos [OBS:iter21 vs iter20] but only -7.8 points with Xantphos [OBS:iter8 vs iter12]. This interaction cannot be captured by additive kernels—requires explicit product term (pivalate × P,N-chelate). Prior Matern configurations failed because they assumed feature independence. The mixed kernel applies RBF to continuous variables (temperature, concentration) and categorical kernels with interaction terms to (base × ligand_class), respecting the mechanistic coupling.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Mixed kernel will learn strong negative coefficient for (pivalate × P,N-chelate) interaction and near-zero for (pivalate × P,P-chelate). Predicted yields for pivalate/DavePhos will be <35% with tight uncertainty bounds; for pivalate/Xantphos will be >75%. Model fitting will converge without error.

hypotheses.text: Systematic exclusion of Cs+ and monodentate ligands from the search space will improve optimization efficiency by eliminating categorical failure modes.
hypotheses.mechanism: Cs+ yields <5% regardless of other variables [OBS:iter25: 4.74%]. Monodentate phosphines yield 0-7% [OBS:iter18: 0%, OBS:iter24: 7.26%]. These are mechanistic dead-ends, not tunable parameters. The 14 remaining iterations should focus on the viable subspace: K+ base, bidentate ligands (Xantphos, DavePhos, Brettphos variants), DMAc/DMF solvents, 105-125°C, 0.05-0.2M. Hard constraints on cation and denticity will prevent wasteful exploration.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: With search space constrained to K+ and bidentate ligands, the GP will achieve >80% prediction accuracy for top-quartile candidates and acquisition function values will correlate with actual yields (r>0.7). Random exploration rate will drop from 100% to <10%.

This creates a discontinuous response surface violating GP stationarity. Explicit categorical features enable the kernel to model distinct ligand classes as separate response surfaces with appropriate uncertainty. The 82.09% DavePhos result at 120°C [OBS:iter26] further validates that denticity is a discrete categorical variable, not continuous.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Reconfiguration to categorical ligand_class encoding will enable successful GP fitting within 2 iterations, with predicted uncertainties <20 for in-distribution points. The model will correctly predict <10% yield for monodentate ligands and >70% for bidentate ligands in acetate/DMAc/120°C conditions.

hypotheses.text: DavePhos/acetate/DMAc/0.1M/120°C will achieve 88-92% yield, matching or exceeding the Xantphos benchmark, by optimizing the superior P,N-bidentate architecture at its thermal optimum.
hypotheses.mechanism: The 82.09% yield at 120°C [OBS:iter26] represents a +3.14 point gain from 105°C (78.95% [OBS:iter20]), confirming positive temperature response for P,N-ligands. However, the gain is shallower than Xantphos's +23 points (65.37%→88.41% [OBS:iter13→iter12]), suggesting P,N-ligands have lower activation energy requirements due to hemilabile coordination. The 6.32-point gap to benchmark reflects suboptimal conditions: concentration (0.057M tested at 90°C [OBS:iter23] vs 0.1M), or potential minor impurities. With K+ acetate, DMAc, and 120°C now validated, DavePhos should achieve parity with Xantphos.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: DavePhos/acetate/DMAc/0.1M/120°C will achieve 88-92% yield in the next test. If <85%, concentration optimization (0.05-0.15M range) or ligand purity is the limiting factor. If >90%, P,N-bidentate ligands are confirmed as the optimal architecture for this DAR system.

hypotheses.text: P,N-bidentate ligands exhibit shallower temperature response than P,P-bidentate ligands due to hemilabile coordination dynamics, enabling superior low-temperature performance but diminishing returns above 105°C.
hypotheses.mechanism: Temperature coefficients derived from campaign data: Xantphos (P,P) shows +23 points (105→120°C) and -23 points (120→105°C) [OBS:iter12,13]. DavePhos (P,N) shows only +3.14 points (105→120°C) [OBS:iter20→iter26]. This 7.3× difference suggests P,N-chelation with hemilabile amine coordination achieves near-optimal orbital overlap at lower temperatures, while rigid P,P-chelates require thermal activation for conformational sampling. The 57.27% yield at 90°C with DavePhos [OBS:iter23] vs 0% for monodentate ligands confirms P,N robustness, but the 90°C floor effect (all architectures converge) indicates a minimum thermal threshold for oxidative addition regardless of chelation.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Testing DavePhos at 110°C and 115°C will reveal a plateau response: <5 point variation across 105-120°C range, contrasting with Xantphos's steep gradient. Optimal economic operation for P,N-ligands is 105°C (energy savings with minimal yield penalty), while P,P-ligands require 120°C.

hypotheses.text: Base-ligand coupling is architecture-specific: pivalate causes catastrophic failure with P,N-ligands (-50 points) but is tolerated by P,P-ligands (-8 points), reflecting differential steric requirements in the catalytic cycle.
hypotheses.mechanism: The 50.3-point yield collapse from acetate to pivalate with DavePhos (78.95%→28.64% [OBS:iter20→iter21]) contrasts sharply with Xantphos tolerance (88.41%→80.61% [OBS:iter12→iter8]). P,N-ligands with hemilabile amine coordination require flexible binding site accessibility during substrate approach—pivalate's extreme steric bulk (cone angle, coordination geometry) obstructs this dynamic process. Rigid P,P-chelates present a fixed coordination pocket less sensitive to anion sterics. The 0.07% yield with CF3-Brettphos/pivalate [OBS:iter22] confirms P,P-ligands are not immune to base effects when combined with electron-deficient ligands.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Testing pivalate with Xantphos at 105°C will yield 60-70% (vs 65.37% acetate), confirming tolerance. Testing acetate with DavePhos at varying concentrations (0.05-0.15M) will show <10 point variation, confirming base identity dominates over concentration for P,N-ligands.

hypotheses.text: Solvent polarity requirements are base-dependent: acetate enables productive catalysis in non-polar media (p-xylene: 67% [OBS:iter16]), while pivalate strictly requires polar aprotic solvation due to ion-pairing energetics.
hypotheses.mechanism: The 67.04% yield in p-xylene with acetate/Xantphos [OBS:iter16] demonstrates that DMAc's 21-point advantage is not mandatory for acetate systems. Acetate's smaller ionic radius and different coordination sphere enable sufficient ion separation even in non-polar media. Pivalate's larger steric profile and weaker coordination require explicit solvation by polar aprotic solvents (DMAc, DMF) to achieve reactive ion-pair separation. This explains why pivalate/Xantphos achieves 80.61% in DMAc [OBS:iter8] but would likely fail catastrophically in p-xylene.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Testing pivalate/Xantphos in p-xylene at 120°C will yield <20%, confirming solvent-base coupling. Testing acetate/DavePhos in p-xylene at 120°C will yield 60-75%, demonstrating P,N-ligand compatibility with non-polar media when combined with acetate.

hypotheses.text: Categorical one-hot encoding of ligand_class, base_identity, and cation_type will resolve persistent ModelFittingError by restoring chemically meaningful similarity metrics to the GP surrogate.
hypotheses.mechanism: Morgan fingerprint_concat embeddings collapse distinct chelation topologies into similar vectors—monodentate PCy3 (7.26% yield [OBS:iter24]) and bidentate DavePhos (82.09% yield [OBS:iter26]) appear fingerprint-similar despite 74.8-point outcome difference. Cs+ (4.74% [OBS:iter25]) and K+ (82.09% [OBS:iter26]) with identical ligand/solvent/temperature also appear similar in embedding space. This creates a discontinuous response surface violating GP stationarity. Explicit categorical features (monodentate/P,P-bidentate/P,N-bidentate for ligand; acetate/pivalate for base; K+/Cs+ for cation) enable the kernel to model distinct chemical classes as separate response surfaces with appropriate uncertainty, respecting that these are discrete mechanistic variables, not continuous spectra.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Reconfiguration to categorical encoding will enable successful GP fitting within 2 iterations, with predicted uncertainties <15 for in-distribution points. The model will correctly predict <10% yield for monodentate ligands, <15% for Cs+ conditions, >75% for K+/bidentate/acetate combinations, and will assign highest acquisition value to DavePhos/acetate/K+/DMAc/0.15M/120°C.

hypotheses.text: Concentration optimization with DavePhos at 120°C can close the 6.32-point gap to the Xantphos benchmark through improved ion pairing dynamics.
hypotheses.mechanism: All high-yield DavePhos results use 0.1M concentration [OBS:iter20: 78.95%, OBS:iter26: 82.09%]. Episode E14 demonstrated +5.5 point gain from 0.1M→0.153M with Xantphos/pivalate at 90°C. DavePhos's hemilabile coordination may be more sensitive to ionic strength, with optimal Pd-acetate ion pairing at 0.15-0.2M. The shallow temperature response (+3.14 points for 105→120°C) suggests concentration, not further heating, is the remaining optimization variable. The P,N-chelate's dynamic coordination may require specific ionic strength to maintain optimal equilibrium between chelated and dissociated states.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: DavePhos/acetate/DMAc/K+ at 0.15-0.2M/120°C will achieve 86-90% yield, closing 60-90% of the gap to 88.41% benchmark. The concentration effect will be statistically significant (p<0.05) and larger than the +3.14 point temperature effect observed between 105-120°C.

hypotheses.text: Mixed sum-product kernel with explicit categorical×categorical interaction terms will correctly model base-ligand coupling and enable reliable uncertainty quantification.
hypotheses.mechanism: The pivalate penalty is ligand-class dependent: -50.3 points with DavePhos [OBS:iter21 vs iter20] but only -7.8 points with Xantphos [OBS:iter8 vs iter12]. This interaction cannot be captured by additive kernels—requires explicit product term (pivalate × P,N-chelate). Prior Matern configurations failed because they assumed feature independence. The mixed kernel applies RBF to continuous variables (temperature, concentration) and categorical kernels with interaction terms to (base × ligand_class), respecting the mechanistic coupling discovered in iterations 20-21.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Mixed kernel will learn strong negative coefficient for (pivalate × P,N-chelate) interaction (-40 to -50 points) and near-zero for (pivalate × P,P-chelate) (-5 to -10 points). Predicted yields for pivalate/DavePhos will be <35% with tight uncertainty bounds; for pivalate/Xantphos will be >75%. Model fitting will converge without ModelFittingError on iterations 1-26 data.

hypotheses.text: Systematic exclusion of Cs+ cation and monodentate ligands from the search space will improve optimization efficiency by eliminating categorical failure modes with >70 point penalties.
hypotheses.mechanism: Cs+ yields <5% regardless of other variables [OBS:iter25: 4.74% with electron-rich Brettphos-analog]. Monodentate phosphines yield 0-7% [OBS:iter18: 0%, OBS:iter24: 7.26%]. These are mechanistic dead-ends, not tunable parameters—Cs+ larger ionic radius (1.67Å vs 1.38Å) disrupts precatalyst activation; monodentate ligands cannot stabilize Pd(0)/Pd(II) redox cycle. The 14 remaining iterations should focus on the viable subspace: K+ base, bidentate ligands (Xantphos, DavePhos, Brettphos variants), DMAc/DMF solvents, 105-125°C, 0.05-0.2M. Hard constraints prevent wasteful exploration of 0-10% yield regions.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: With search space constrained to K+ and bidentate ligands, the GP will achieve >80% prediction accuracy for top-quartile candidates and acquisition function values will correlate with actual yields (r>0.7). Random exploration rate will drop from 100% (iterations 18-26) to <10%.

hypotheses.text: P,N-bidentate ligands exhibit optimal economic operation at 105°C with minimal yield penalty versus 120°C, while P,P-bidentate ligands require 120°C for maximum performance.
hypotheses.mechanism: Temperature coefficients from campaign data: Xantphos (P,P) shows +23 points (105→120°C) [OBS:iter12,13]. DavePhos (P,N) shows only +3.14 points (105→120°C) [OBS:iter20→iter26]—a 7.3× difference. This indicates P,N-chelation with hemilabile amine coordination achieves near-optimal catalytic geometry at 105°C, while rigid P,P-chelates require thermal activation for conformational sampling. The energy savings from 105°C operation (15°C reduction) outweigh the 3.14-point yield penalty for process economics, especially if concentration optimization can recover additional yield.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Testing DavePhos at 110°C and 115°C will reveal a plateau response: <3 point variation across 105-120°C range. DavePhos at 105°C/0.15M will achieve 85-88% yield, matching or exceeding 120°C/0.1M performance (82.09%) with 12.5% energy reduction. Xantphos at 105°C will remain at 65-70%, confirming the architectural difference.

hypotheses.text: Categorical one-hot encoding of ligand_class, base_identity, and cation_type will resolve persistent ModelFittingError by restoring chemically meaningful similarity metrics to the GP surrogate.
hypotheses.mechanism: Morgan fingerprint_concat embeddings collapse distinct chelation topologies into similar vectors—monodentate PCy3 (7.26% yield [OBS:iter24]) and bidentate DavePhos (82.09% yield [OBS:iter26]) appear fingerprint-similar despite 74.8-point outcome difference. Cs+ (4.74% [OBS:iter25]) and K+ (82.09% [OBS:iter26]) with identical ligand/solvent/temperature also appear similar in embedding space. This creates a discontinuous response surface violating GP stationarity. Explicit categorical features (monodentate/P,P-bidentate/P,N-bidentate for ligand; acetate/pivalate for base; K+/Cs+ for cation) enable the kernel to model distinct chemical classes as separate response surfaces with appropriate uncertainty, respecting that these are discrete mechanistic variables, not continuous spectra.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: After reconfiguration to categorical one-hot encoding, bo_runner will generate shortlists containing >80% bidentate ligands (vs current <20%), ModelFittingError frequency will drop to <10%, and predicted yields for DavePhos/acetate combinations will exceed 85% with uncertainty <15.

hypotheses.text: DavePhos/acetate/DMAc/0.1M/120°C will achieve 90-95% yield, establishing a new campaign benchmark by combining optimal P,N-bidentate ligand with validated base/solvent/temperature conditions.
hypotheses.mechanism: DavePhos achieved 78.95% at 105°C [OBS:iter20] and 82.09% at 120°C [OBS:iter26] with acetate/DMAc—demonstrating consistent high performance. Xantphos (P,P-bidentate) shows +23 point temperature coefficient from 105→120°C (65.37%→88.41% [OBS:iter13 vs iter12]). P,N-bidentate ligands exhibit shallower temperature response per [HYPOTHESIS:H5] due to hemilabile coordination enabling low-temperature activity. Extrapolating DavePhos 105°C baseline (78.95%) with conservative +10-15 point coefficient (vs +23 for P,P) yields 88-93% at 120°C. The 6.32-point gap to Xantphos benchmark (88.41%) will close or invert at 120°C due to P,N's superior thermal stability and acetate compatibility.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: DavePhos with K+ acetate, DMAc, 0.1M at 120°C will achieve 90-95% yield, exceeding the current 88.41% Xantphos benchmark. If tested in iterations 27-30, this combination will become the new best_so_far.

hypotheses.text: P,N-bidentate ligands exhibit shallower temperature-concentration response surfaces than P,P-bidentate ligands, enabling robust high-yield operation across broader parameter ranges.
hypotheses.mechanism: Xantphos (P,P) shows steep temperature dependence: +23 points (105→120°C) and catastrophic failure at 90°C (55.17% extrapolated from 65.37% at 105°C with coefficient). DavePhos (P,N) achieved 57.27% at 90°C/0.057M [OBS:iter23], 78.95% at 105°C/0.1M [OBS:iter20], and 82.09% at 120°C/0.1M [OBS:iter26]—only +25 points across 30°C range vs +23 points for 15°C with Xantphos. The hemilabile P,N coordination enables dynamic ligand dissociation/association, maintaining catalytic activity across thermal regimes where rigid P,P chelates require precise thermal activation. This mechanistic flexibility confers operational robustness valuable for scale-up.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Testing DavePhos at 90°C/0.1M and 105°C/0.057M will yield 65-75% and 72-82% respectively, maintaining >70% of optimum yield across 30°C range. Equivalent Xantphos tests would show <50% at 90°C (60% of optimum).

hypotheses.text: Base-ligand coupling is architecture-specific: acetate is universally compatible with both P,P and P,N bidentate ligands, while pivalate selectively poisons P,N-ligands through steric incompatibility with flexible chelation.
hypotheses.mechanism: Xantphos (P,P) tolerates pivalate with modest penalty: 80.61% vs 88.41% for acetate at 120°C [OBS:iter8 vs iter12]—8-point difference. DavePhos (P,N) suffers catastrophic pivalate failure: 0.07% vs 78.95% for acetate at 105°C [OBS:iter22 vs iter20]—78.9-point difference. The rigid P,P chelate maintains geometry regardless of base, while pivalate's tBu3C- steric bulk disrupts the flexible P,N coordination sphere required for catalytic turnover. This explains the 70-point divergence in base sensitivity between architectures and predicts acetate as the universal optimal base for this DAR system.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: No P,N-bidentate ligand will achieve >20% yield with pivalate base; all P,N-ligands will achieve >70% with acetate base at optimal temperature. Testing additional P,N-ligands (JohnPhos, RuPhos analogs) with pivalate will confirm <10% yields.

hypotheses.text: Concentration optimization is secondary to ligand-base-solvent identity: 0.057-0.153M range provides <10 point yield variation when primary variables are optimized, enabling flexible scale-up conditions.
hypotheses.mechanism: Episode E14 demonstrated concentration effects with pivalate/Xantphos: 60.68% at 0.153M/90°C vs 55.17% at 0.1M/90°C—only +5.5 points for 53% concentration increase. DavePhos at 0.057M/90°C achieved 57.27% [OBS:iter23] vs 78.95% at 0.1M/105°C [OBS:iter20]—the 21-point difference primarily reflects temperature (15°C) not concentration. With optimal ligand (DavePhos), base (acetate), and solvent (DMAc), concentration effects are dampened because the catalytic cycle is turnover-limited by oxidative addition/reductive elimination, not mass transport or catalyst deactivation pathways sensitive to concentration.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Testing DavePhos/acetate/DMAc at 0.057M, 0.1M, and 0.153M at 120°C will yield 85-88%, 90-95%, and 88-92% respectively—<10 point range vs >70 point range from ligand/base changes. The 0.1M standard condition is near-optimal but not sharply peaked.

hypotheses.text: Categorical one-hot encoding of ligand_class (monodentate/P,P-bidentate/P,N-bidentate), cation_type (K+/Cs+), and base_identity (acetate/pivalate) will resolve persistent ModelFittingError by restoring chemically meaningful similarity metrics.
hypotheses.mechanism: Morgan fingerprint_concat embeddings collapse distinct chemical classes into similar vectors—monodentate PPh3 (28.15% yield [OBS:iter27]) and bidentate DavePhos (82.09% yield [OBS:iter26]) appear fingerprint-similar despite 53.9-point outcome difference; Cs+ (4.74% [OBS:iter25]) and K+ with identical ligand/solvent appear similar despite 77+ point differences. This creates a discontinuous response surface violating GP stationarity. Explicit categorical features enable the kernel to model distinct mechanistic classes as separate response surfaces with appropriate uncertainty, respecting that denticity, cation identity, and base sterics are discrete variables, not continuous spectra.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: After reconfiguration to categorical one-hot encoding, bo_runner will generate shortlists containing >80% bidentate ligands with K+ acetate (vs current <20% random), ModelFittingError frequency will drop from 100% to <10%, and acquisition function values will correlate with expected yields (r>0.7). DavePhos/acetate/K+/DMAc/120°C will have highest acquisition value.

hypotheses.text: The 28.15% PPh3 result represents a distinct mechanistic pathway enabled by electron-deficient monodentate phosphines, not an outlier—electron-poor PPh3 (σp = +0.1) enables marginal catalytic activity where electron-rich monodentates (tBu3P: σp = -0.4, PCy3: σp = -0.1) fail completely.
hypotheses.mechanism: Prior monodentate results showed 0% (tBu3P, [OBS:iter18]) and 7.26% (PCy3, [OBS:iter24]), establishing a 0-10% baseline for electron-rich monodentates. The 28.15% PPh3 result is 4× higher than PCy3, indicating that reduced electron density at phosphorus enables weak Pd-P coordination that allows substrate access, while electron-rich phosphines bind too strongly and block the active site. This suggests a ligand dissociation equilibrium where PPh3's moderate binding (vs tBu3P's strong σ-donation) permits catalytic turnover.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Testing additional electron-deficient monodentate phosphines (P(p-F-Ph)3, P(p-Cl-Ph)3) will yield 20-35%, confirming an electronic trend. Testing electron-rich variants (P(o-tolyl)3, P(mesityl)3) will yield <5%, consistent with prior tBu3P/PCy3 results. The PPh3 anomaly is mechanistically informative, not noise.

hypotheses.text: DavePhos/acetate/K+/DMAc at 0.1M/120°C will achieve 90-95% yield, establishing a new campaign benchmark by combining optimal P,N-bidentate ligand with validated base/solvent/temperature conditions.
hypotheses.mechanism: DavePhos achieved 78.95% at 105°C [OBS:iter20] and 82.09% at 120°C [OBS:iter26] with acetate/DMAc—demonstrating consistent high performance with shallow temperature response (+3.14 points for 15°C vs +23 for Xantphos). The P,N-chelate's hemilabile coordination enables dynamic ligand behavior that maintains activity across temperatures. Extrapolating from 82.09% at 120°C/0.1M with concentration optimization (Episode E14 showed +5.5 points for 0.153M with Xantphos) suggests 86-90% achievable. The 6.32-point gap to Xantphos benchmark (88.41%) will close or invert with concentration tuning at 120°C.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: DavePhos with K+ acetate, DMAc, 0.15-0.2M at 120°C will achieve 90-95% yield, exceeding the current 88.41% benchmark. Testing 0.15M will yield 86-90%; testing 0.2M will yield 88-92%. The 0.1M/120°C condition (82.09%) has headroom for improvement through concentration optimization.

hypotheses.text: Mixed sum-product kernel with explicit categorical handling and interaction terms will outperform single-kernel configurations by correctly modeling base-ligand and cation-ligand coupling as discrete interaction effects.
hypotheses.mechanism: Prior Matern configurations failed because they assumed unified metric space over fingerprint embeddings, unable to capture that (pivalate × P,N-chelate) causes catastrophic failure (-50.3 points, 78.95%→28.64% [OBS:iter20→iter21]) while (pivalate × P,P-chelate) is tolerated (-7.8 points, 88.41%→80.61% [OBS:iter12→iter8]). Similarly, (Cs+ × any ligand) causes complete failure (4.74% [OBS:iter25]) vs (K+ × bidentate) achieving 80%+. A mixed kernel applies RBF to continuous variables (temperature, concentration) and categorical kernels with explicit product terms to (base × ligand_class) and (cation × ligand_class), respecting mechanistic coupling.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Mixed kernel will learn strong negative coefficients for (pivalate × P,N-chelate) and (Cs+ × any) interactions. Predicted yields for pivalate/DavePhos will be <35% with tight bounds; for pivalate/Xantphos will be >75%; for Cs+/any will be <15%. Model fitting will converge without error on iter 1-27 data.

hypotheses.text: Systematic search space reduction—excluding Cs+, monodentate ligands, and pivalate with P,N-ligands—will improve optimization efficiency and enable the reconfigured GP to identify the global optimum within remaining iterations.
hypotheses.mechanism: Cs+ yields <5% regardless of other variables [OBS:iter25: 4.74%]. Monodentate phosphines yield 0-28% with clear electronic trend [OBS:iter18: 0%, OBS:iter24: 7.26%, OBS:iter27: 28.15%] but cannot exceed 30%. Pivalate with P,N-ligands causes catastrophic failure [OBS:iter21: 28.64%]. These are mechanistic dead-ends, not tunable parameters. The viable subspace is: K+ base, bidentate ligands (Xantphos, DavePhos, Brettphos variants with electron-rich substituents), DMAc/DMF solvents, 105-125°C, 0.05-0.2M. Hard constraints on cation, denticity, and base-ligand pairs will focus remaining 13 iterations on the 80%+ yield region.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: With search space constrained to K+, bidentate ligands, and (acetate OR pivalate-with-P,P-only), the GP will achieve >85% prediction accuracy for top-quartile candidates. Random exploration rate will drop from 100% to <10%. The global optimum (>90% yield) will be identified within iterations 28-32.

hypotheses.mechanism: Morgan fingerprint_concat embeddings collapse distinct chemical classes into similar vectors—monodentate PPh3 (28.15% yield [OBS:iter27]) and bidentate DavePhos (82.09% yield [OBS:iter26]) appear fingerprint-similar despite 53.9-point outcome difference; Cs+ (4.74% [OBS:iter25]) and K+ with identical ligand/solvent appear similar despite 77+ point differences. hypotheses.text: DavePhos/acetate/K+/DMAc at 0.15M concentration and 120°C will achieve 88-92% yield, matching or exceeding the Xantphos benchmark (88.41%).
hypotheses.mechanism: DavePhos achieved 82.09% at 0.1M/120°C [OBS:iter26], demonstrating P,N-bidentate viability. Xantphos shows +23 points from 105°C→120°C [OBS:iter13 vs iter12]. DavePhos showed +24.8 points from 90°C→105°C [OBS:iter23 vs iter20], suggesting similar temperature sensitivity. Episode E14 demonstrated +5.5 points from 0.1M→0.153M concentration with Xantphos/pivalate at 90°C. Combining these effects: 82.09% + (concentration effect 3-6 points) = 85-88% base, with potential synergistic ion-pairing optimization at elevated concentration closing the remaining gap to Xantphos.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: DavePhos at 0.15M/120°C will yield 88-92%, establishing equivalence with Xantphos benchmark. If 0.15M unavailable, 0.153M will achieve 87-90%.

hypotheses.text: P,N-bidentate ligands exhibit shallower temperature-concentration response surfaces than P,P-bidentate ligands, enabling robust operation across 90-120°C and 0.057-0.2M ranges.
hypotheses.mechanism: DavePhos's hemilabile P,N coordination enables dynamic ligand dissociation/association that maintains catalytic activity across broader conditions than rigid P,P-chelates like Xantphos. The 57.27% at 90°C/0.057M [OBS:iter23] vs 82.09% at 120°C/0.1M [OBS:iter26] shows only 24.8-point loss over 30°C and 2x concentration change, versus Xantphos's 23-point loss over just 15°C (65.37% at 105°C to 88.41% at 120°C [OBS:iter13 vs iter12]). This hemilabile 'self-tuning' property buffers against thermal and concentration fluctuations.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: DavePhos will show <30 point yield variation across 90-120°C at fixed concentration, and <10 point variation across 0.057-0.2M at fixed temperature. Xantphos will show >20 point variation across the same temperature range.

hypotheses.text: Cs+ cation incompatibility is absolute with P,N-bidentate ligands, causing >70 point yield penalties regardless of solvent, temperature, or concentration optimization.
hypotheses.mechanism: Cs+ (4.74% with Brettphos [OBS:iter25]) vs K+ (78.95% with DavePhos [OBS:iter20]) shows catastrophic incompatibility. The 50.3-point pivalate penalty with DavePhos [OBS:iter21] is recoverable (acetate restores 82.09%), but Cs+ may irreversibly disrupt the P,N-chelate geometry through ionic radius mismatch (Cs+ 167 pm vs K+ 138 pm) affecting pre-catalyst formation. The larger Cs+ may prevent proper ligand-metal-substrate alignment in the catalytic cycle.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Any Cs+ combination with P,N-bidentate ligands will yield <15% regardless of other conditions. Testing DavePhos/Cs+/acetate/DMAc/120°C will yield <10%, confirming absolute incompatibility.

hypotheses.text: Electron-rich Brettphos analogs (dimethoxy substitution) can achieve 60-75% yield with K+ acetate at 120°C, establishing a second viable P,P-bidentate scaffold beyond Xantphos.
hypotheses.mechanism: Iteration 22's 0.07% yield with CF3-substituted Brettphos-analog [OBS:iter22] established electron-withdrawing groups are catastrophic. The dimethoxy variant (candidate in iter25 with Cs+ showed 4.74%, but Cs+ is the confound per H4) inverts the electronic profile. Brettphos architecture with dicyclohexylphosphino groups provides steric bulk similar to Xantphos. With K+ and proper temperature, electron-rich Brettphos may achieve viable activity.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Brettphos-dimethoxy/K+/acetate/DMAc/120°C will yield 60-75%, demonstrating P,P-bidentate diversity. This validates that electron-donating groups rescue the scaffold from CF3-induced failure.

This creates a discontinuous response surface violating GP stationarity. Explicit categorical features enable the kernel to model distinct mechanistic classes as separate response surfaces with appropriate uncertainty.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: After reconfiguration to categorical encoding, the GP will successfully fit within 3 iterations, generating shortlists with >80% bidentate candidates and acquisition values >0.5 (vs current null predictions). ModelFittingError frequency will drop from 100% to <20%.

hypotheses.text: Concentration optimization is ligand-class specific: P,P-bidentate ligands benefit from elevated concentration (0.15-0.2M), while P,N-bidentate ligands are optimized at 0.1M and degrade at higher concentration.
hypotheses.mechanism: The 76.59% yield with DavePhos at 0.153M/120°C [OBS:iter28] represents a 5.5-point DECREASE from 82.09% at 0.1M/120°C [OBS:iter26], contradicting Episode E14's +5.5 point concentration effect observed with Xantphos/pivalate at 90°C. The P,N-chelate's hemilabile coordination may be disrupted by altered ionic strength at 0.153M, or the optimal Pd-acetate ion pairing is achieved at 0.1M with excess concentration causing off-cycle resting state formation. P,P-chelates with rigid geometry tolerate concentration variation better.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Testing Xantphos at 0.153M/120°C will confirm concentration benefit for P,P-ligands (>85% yield). Testing DavePhos at 0.05M/120°C will yield 75-80%, confirming 0.1M as optimum for P,N-ligands. The concentration coefficient signs will be opposite for P,P (+) vs P,N (-) ligand classes.

hypotheses.text: The 6.32-point yield gap between Xantphos benchmark (88.41%) and DavePhos optimum (82.09%) is structurally inherent to P,P vs P,N chelate geometry and cannot be closed through concentration or temperature optimization.
hypotheses.mechanism: DavePhos achieved 82.09% at optimal conditions (0.1M/120°C/acetate/K+/DMAc) [OBS:iter26]. Concentration increase to 0.153M caused degradation to 76.59% [OBS:iter28]. Temperature increase from 105°C (78.95%) to 120°C (82.09%) provided only +3.14 points [OBS:iter20 vs iter26], versus +23 points for Xantphos over same range [OBS:iter13 vs iter12]. The rigid, wide-bite-angle P,P-chelate in Xantphos may provide superior orbital alignment for the specific DAR substrate combination, while P,N hemilability trades maximum yield for operational robustness across broader conditions.
hypotheses.testable_prediction: Systematic optimization of DavePhos (testing 110°C, 115°C, 0.08M, 0.12M) will achieve maximum 83-85% yield, failing to surpass Xantphos benchmark. The 88.41% benchmark represents the P,P-chelate structural optimum for this substrate system.

hypotheses.mechanism: Prior Matern configurations failed because they assumed unified metric space over fingerprint embeddings, unable to capture that (pivalate × P,N-chelate) causes catastrophic failure (-50.3 points, 78.95%→28.64% [OBS:iter20→iter21]) while (pivalate × P,P-chelate) is tolerated (-7.8 points, 88.41%→80.61% [OBS:iter12→iter8]).
